content.value: I am a Filipina-Italian-American multi-media artist from San Jose, CA. I am a graduate of the University of Southern California’s School of Cinematic Arts, majoring in
Media Arts + Practice

with a double minor in Dance and Computer Programming. I am currently a Product Designer at Apple and take on freelance photography in both digital and film mediums (clients include: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts & Sciences, USC Kaufman School of Dance, BODYTRAFFIC).

Since I was little, I've had a passion for performing arts while simultaneously growing skills in media technologies. Through my studies and work experience, I have gained a rich knowledge of interaction design and storytelling techniques, retaining an earnest curiosity in combining areas of movement, media technologies, and design.

My wide-range of skills can be attributed to my insatiable desire to understand the world around us, and how technology can influence how we live. As a photographer, dancer, and designer, I have gained a strong visual understanding. I believe visual storytelling is the most effective way to impact others through unique and imaginative perspectives. I value play and take each project as an opportunity to creatively experiment different dimensions of storytelling – from user stories to stories of my own voice.

In all the work I do, I think about the potential impact on humanity as a whole, retaining a passionate awareness of underserved communities and the future of our planet. I am excited about the potential for experience design to solve humanity's problems in creative ways. I am always open to new opportunities and projects where I can gain new skills as well as apply my own. If you're interested in collaborating, hiring, or just chatting about life, feel free to send me an email!
Overview

On July 15, 2021, we launched the Magnolia App, a joint-venture from Chip & Joanna Gaines and Discovery Inc that is a digital home for all things Magnolia. As a product designer on a Design team of 10 people, I helped in the creation of this app across multiple platforms including web, iOS, Android, tvOS, Roku, and FireTV. The content within the app is organized into 4 verticals – Watch, Shop, Visit, and Create. I aided in all aspects of product design such as ideation, research, prototyping, pitching, and QAing. I worked closely with my design manager, senior designers, and product managers to establish requirements and deliver final designs.
Prototyping & Motion Design

One of my primary responsibilities on the team was creating prototypes and motion design assets for pitching purposes and deliverables for engineering. When building prototypes, I would work with the senior designers to understand the user journey + interactions to create a storyboard of the prototype. From there I would build the prototype using various tools at my fingertips – Sketch, Figma, Principle and After Effects.

Below is an example of a prototype I created for a pitch deck that proposed a creation of a "Day/Night mode" for our CTV app (demonstrated on tvOS). Here, the user is greeted with a message dependent on the time they open the app. The background of Home is then dark or light to reflect the time of day they are browsing. As the user scrolls down the page, they encounter full-bleed "disruptors" that break up the continuous scroll of shelves. This prototype was presented in front of Joanna Gaines for approval.
Day Mode Prototype for Magnolia App on tvOS. Please contact me for full high-res video.

Another prototype I created was for the onboarding experience of the Magnolia App. This involved several rounds of iterating in order to create the most optimal, engaging, and on-brand interaction. Creating an interactive prototype was essential in the decision-making process, as it promoted empathy for the users.
An iteration of a prototype for the mobile onboarding of the Magnolia App on iOS. Please contact me for full high-res video.

With the deadline for launch quickly approaching, I was challenged to lead the improvement of the motion of our app while we were in the QA-phase of the product in the months before launch. With the goal of making the interactions and transitions as clean and premium as possible, I worked with the developers to implement these improvements — which included loading-states, hover-states, and interactive component animations. These were primarily communicated through Jira tickets that I wrote referencing already-existing motion libraries and custom prototypes made by myself. I would also participate in virtual 1:1 working sessions with the developers to achieve the desired look & feel. Below is a capture of a spreadsheet I created to track the various motion improvements cross-platform.

One of my favorite responsibilities was creating "sizzle reels" for our app that would be used by the VP of Design and the General Manager of Magnolia DTC to communicate our efforts to Discovery leadership and other departments within the company. Creating the sizzles gave me an opportunity to understand the scope of the projects the entire Magnolia DTC team worked on. I would work with my VP to determine features to showcase and choose a song that worked with the story we wanted to tell, which I would cut using Adobe Audition. I would then storyboard the sizzle reel by breaking down the song to its emotional cues and present visual stills of the design assets choreographed to music. Once approved, I would build the motion entirely using After Effects and make edits based off notes I received from my VP. At the top of this page is the sizzle reel I created with the help of a visual designer on our team to celebrate the launch of our app.
Sizzle Reel storyboard process
Create Vertical

As a product designer, I was responsible for owning certain features, most notably Create on mobile app & web. This was my first experience designing a feature that was part of a product built entirely from scratch. I worked collaboratively with the feature-owners of the other verticals to ensure design consistency, with Product to understand the overarching business and customer goals, and User Research to learn user expectations and assumptions.

The biggest challenge of this feature was that throughout my design process, the feature requirements were still being defined by Product and Magnolia leadership (the name "Create" wasn't even given to it until soon before launch after our user research team conducted multiple tests and interviews). Because of this, I started my design process by first understanding the Magnolia brand and audience. I digested Magnolia audience research documents and put myself in the shoes of a Magnolia fan by following their socials, launch campaigns, and re-watching episodes of Fixer Upper. I also conducted competitive analysis of near-competitors that I understood the "Create" vertical was taking influence from, based on the loose definitions of incorporating brand-new Magnolia Workshops and incorporating their existing Blog. I worked with a senior designer to create a deck to compare how services like Masterclass, Pinterest, Food Network Kitchen, Goop, etc. presented their services/content.

To help define this vertical, I also worked with our User Research team to determine the most comprehensible navigational architecture. I participated in defining testing goals and provided white-label assets for the tests. Our research team ran 2 tests – one to cast a large net on the variety of titles for this verticle (which included a card-sorting and ranking exercise) and another to understand user's expectations and associations based on certain names and provided UI. From the 2 tests, "Create" was considered the top result with Workshops, Recipes, and Blogs falling as content types underneath it. After presenting these findings to Magnolia leadership, they approved the naming of the vertical as "Create" with the subsequent content types for launch.
Results for 2 user tests ran on usertesting.com that were digested and presented to us by our User Research team

One limitation for designing this feature was that we would be building our app using an internal Discovery tool that was created for another app within Discovery's portfolio that launched prior. This means that there were content-management building blocks in place that the design had to support. Because the navigational architecture of this vertical was not finalized, I designed with flexible components in mind. Below is a preliminary wireframe I sketched to visualize how these components could be presented for the Create homepage.

After wireframing and getting a better understanding of the content that would be displayed in this vertical based on research and conversations with Product, I comped up Create Home designs for approval. I re-purposed to my advantage what was already delivered for other verticals and thus was available in our "design system" (such as ledes, navigational blocks, full-bleed promos, and rails of cards) as well as created my own based on the proposed content types that were exclusive to my vertical (workshops, recipes, blog posts, photo galleries, etc). Because my comps were going to be used for pitch-purposes, I paid heavy attention to the visual imagery and variety in components to inspire Magnolia in the flexibility of this page. Below is my first iteration that I pitched to Magnolia leadership.

Magnolia received these designs favorably; however, they asked us to explore options for the lede area, as they wanted the Create vertical to be distinguishable from the other verticals. I worked with a designer whose main responsibility was creating illustrative icons to pitch different creative concepts for the Create homepage, as seen below. You can see what they went with for launch on the live website
here
(in addition to Create Home I also owned Recipes and Blog).
Design System

As a brand-new design team of about 10 designing a product from scratch fully-remote in the middle of a pandemic, it was essential that we had a process in place to keep our designs consistent from designer to platform to vertical. We had weekly library syncs to update each other on decisions made for the various styles and components of our app. When we first started, we utilized Sketch libraries and Zeplin to collaborate and deliver to development. After a year, we eventually transitioned to Figma, which greatly aided in our collaboration.

As the designer for the Create vertical, I was responsible for organizing cards of the various content types that the vertical included. I worked with my product manager to define the asset requirements, such as the metadata available and when/where certain metadata would be displayed.

Once our app launched, it became a greater priorty for Product, Design, and Development to re-think the card component to be more flexible, unified, and consistent throughout our app. This led to a card exercise I embarked on in Figma to create a "master card" component that could be applied to all of our 7+ content types (Episode, Show, Workshop, Course, Recipe, Blog, Product, Talent). This exercise allowed me to learn the ins and outs of Figma libraries, being my first opportunity to do so. I learned to build the complex "master card" component using atomic base-components, which allowed for flexibility in metadata, aspect ratio, and states.

After creating the master card, I used this as a blueprint to make our main card component that has variants for content type, breakpoint, and dark vs. light mode. I then presented this component to the entire team during our weekly library sync, and we now use this component in our design system. We are currently in the process of shifting our product to component-based design on the engineering-side, ideally making our product more flexible & efficient with a consistent user experience.
Overview

At my time at NBC, I led the UX/UI of the Voice App for Season 18 & 19 and America's Got Talent App for Season 14 & 15. These two apps are paired with the broadcasted episodes for play-along experiences such as voting, building fantasy teams, and answering trivia/polls for points.

At the beginning of each season's timeline I would work with our Product team to prioritize improvements and features to incorporate into the upcoming season. These would be identified through understanding the personas of those who watch The Voice and AGT while using the play-along app, disecting the user journey, auditing the app's accessibility against current standards, and reading through the app store reviews. Once these improvements/features were identified, I would begin my design process. This included conducting an analysis of other products with similar interactions, sketching out possible user flows and interfaces, obtaining feedback through design reviews, iterating these designs, creating prototypes, executing user tests, working with product to identify user data points, pitching my ideas to executives, and creating spec documents of these deliverables.
America's Got Talent - Design Process

An example of a recent feature I worked on was the Congratulations screen for the America's Got Talent app. This screen appears at the end of the season once a winner is announced and remains present till the start of the next season. After analyzing our app store reviews, a user painpoint was discovered. Because the Congratulations screen is triggered right after the winner is announced, many users would be spoiled of the AGT results when opening the app before actually completing the final episode (say, if they were planning to watch the episode after its broadcast). In addition, we found that this screen could be more beneficial to users who use the app in-between seasons, sharing more relevant and up-to-date information. After discussing with the Product team, we created a requirement to allow for customizeable promo cards in addition to announcing the winner for this screen.
Screenshot of the Congratulations screen for Season 14, prior to re-design

Visually, I found this to be a unique and exciting design challenge. After browsing through inspiration, I concepted various ways that users could interact with this screen to reveal who the winner is. As a brainstorming exercise, I wireframed by hand the possible different states of this screen, allowing an area for promo cards with customizeable copy.
Wireframes sketched by hand

After conceptualizing a few variations of this screen, I brought my designs into Sketch. I approached my initial designs as a playground to explore aesthetic possibilities. I went through several rounds of iteration to visualize styling for the revealing the winner interaction and the promotional cards. I used various AGT assets, such as iconography and show art, as inspiration for adding branding details and texture to this screen. I thought about how animation could be used to add delight to these screens as well.
Screenshot of my Sketch file

Before deciding on which options to create prototypes for, I went through multiple rounds of peer reviews with members of my team to get their impressions and advice on usability, accessibility, and aesthetics. I incorporated their notes and settled on my top two options to bring into Principle & After Effects to visualize how this interaction would look like.

I took these designs to the VP of UX Design for review. Option 1 was preferred because of how it directed the focus of the user with a clear separation of content and its enjoyable interaction that encouraged more engagement. As we were designing for an entertainment app, delightful design is valued greatly.

Once approved by the VP of UX Design, I walked through the chosen design with Product to get their alignment. From there we did a final review with the executive team of our department. Once approved, I delivered the final designs through Zeplin and spec-ed out the animation for our development team. There were three rounds of Design QA in the timeline for the Beta, Alpha, and Release Candidate builds, where I logged bug tickets to ensure that this screen was developed as intended and covered all technical edge-cases. It was released for America's Got Talent Season 15 App.
Final product
Overview

In June 2019, the NBC App launched a re-design that incorporated 11 of NBC's brand apps—including Bravo, E!, USA, SYFY, Oxygen, and Telemundo—with a consistent user experience across all apps and 10+ platforms. Upon its launch, the NBC App was recognized as "Best in Class tvOS App" and is referenced in Apple's Human Interface Guidelines for Navigation, Search, and Live schedule.
View
here

As a team of about 10 UX designers, we worked collaboratively across various features and platforms, utilizing a breadth of interdiscplinary skills. I had the opportunity to design UX/UI for cross-platform experiences—including iOS, Android, tvOS, Roku, Fire TV, and Web—using Sketch, Zeplin, Principle, Photoshop, and After Effects. Each of us were expected to administer competitive analyses on competing apps/services every quarter, conduct quantitative and qualitative user research, create and test high-fidelity prototypes, work closely with product team to track issues and design solutions for updates or bugs, collaborate with the marketing team to design app screens, icons, and other promotional assets for iTunes and Google Play stores, pitch design concepts to executives for approval, and detail spec docs and user flows to account for all scenarios and use cases.

Beyond our existing responsibilities and expectations, I specifically honed in on creating prototypes and sizzle videos for pitches, ad sales demos, and company townhalls (utilizing Principle and After Effects). Above is the sizzle video created for the June 2019 launch of the NBC App and brand apps.
Competitive Analyses

An ongoing job responsibility for launching and updating the NBC streaming apps was conducting competitive analyses of our competitors' new/updated products and subsequent features. Every quarter we would individually be assigned to conduct an analysis of a specific product, feauture, or interface. For the launch of HBO Max in June 2020, I presented a competitive analysis of this product (entertainingly in the theme of Mad Max) during our team's quarterly all-hands meeting of about 125 people. Contact me for more information on these decks.
Motion Design

In addition to designing static UI, one of my skillsets was to illustrate the movement of our interfaces when ideating, pitching, iterating, and delivering through high-fidelity prototypes. I was tasked to visualize movement of our interfaces across all our platforms (mobile, TV, and web). These were created using Principle and After Effects.

Below is an example of a prototype I created to conceptualize the movement within NBC App on tvOS. It shows the user navigating to the Networks shelf, where each brand has their own tile animation when in focus. The user then clicks into the Oxygen brand and is redirected to the Oxygen brand-landing page. A video background unique to the brand animates over their selected gradient color as the content floats and fades in. When the user hovers over the first tile, they are shown a video preview of that show.

image.title: NBC App - TVOS - Home to Brand Landing Prototype
image.description: NBC App - TVOS - Home to Brand Landing Prototype

content.value: NBC App prototype on tvOS

Another example below is a prototype I created for the NBC App on iOS. It visualizes the flow of a feature that I was tasked to re-design. The Previews shelf on mobile allows users to watch trailers of new shows, in the same vein as watching Instagram stories. The prototype shows a user tapping on one of these previews on the homepage. They then swipe through the previews of various shows before tapping on the Start Watching CTA, where they are then redirected to the video player of that episode.
NBC App prototype on iOS

In addition to prototyping UI transitions, I would also produce animation assets for both internal and external use. Below is an example of an animation I created to visualize the NBC logo transitioning to the logo for Peacock, NBC's new streaming service, using After Effects. To conceptualize this motion, I compared how the new Peacock logo related to the distinct NBC logo. It consists of a "spine" of dots that each borrow the colors from the NBC peacock rainbow. Mimicking how a peacock "fans out" its feathers, I imagined the NBC logo unfurling and the motion continuing to spin around as the feathers turn into dots that eventually stack onto its spine form. I went through multiple iterations and reviews to smooth out the motion and make it as seemless as possible. At its final form, this animation visualizes the relationship of the fresh product of Peacock to NBC as a distinguished brand.
Accessibility
Because quality UX is an experience that works for
all

users, an essential part of our workflow as UX designers was ensuring our designs followed accessibility standards outlined by W3C and NBCUniversal's Accessibility Director. The features I designed consciously followed these guidelines and would be tested and reviewed to ensure its usability. In addition, I worked on several features specifically created to enhance the accessibility of our products. This required an understanding of the complexities of audio, visual, or motor limitations, and the variants in user experiences for those with such disabilities.

For example, users who are tabbing through our website and/or engaging with screen readers are used to a pattern of navigation where a "Skip to Content" button exists that allows the focus to shift past the navigation options to the main content of the page. For NBC.com, I incorporated this Skip to Content pattern that appears when users first tab through the website.
Overview
Above is the submission video for UserTesting's Illumini 2020 awards that I created. The NBC app was awarded in the Summer of 2020.

NBCUniversal’s Identity program launched in the Fall of 2017 on NBC Broadcast Network’s digital apps and website, and has since expanded across multiple cable brands including Bravo, USA, Oxygen, Syfy, Telemundo and E!. Since launch, its UX has iterated, enhanced, and evolved to fit the ever-changing industry and landscape. With the help of UserTesting, we've run a wide range of tests to inform how to optimize ease of use and bring maximum value for our users. Having valid, valuable user data has helped us design more optimal user flows, improved our user journeys, and enhanced our overall user experiences ultimately leading to increased registration, engagement and conversion.
The Voice & America's Got Talent

As NBCUniversal's Identity transformed through various iterations—such as adding multiple sign-in methods, creating more accessible form-fields, and general usability improvements—it was my responsibility to translate these updates as the lead designer for The Voice and America's Got Talent App. Every season, the specific integration of Identity within these apps would also be updated to reflect the season's show art. I would create these designs, review them with executives, and compile the final comps and assets to deliver to our development team, using Sketch and Zeplin.
Involvement
In the fall of 2018 I worked with one other UX designer under the guidance of the digital production manager and senior marketing manager of the D23: The Official Disney Fan Club team.
Goal
Explore how
D23.com
can improve the "Membership Information" page to successfully and pleasantly market their membership.
Identify
pain points
of current D23 Membership Information page
Competitively analyze
the online presence of other Disney services
Compare & contrast
online presence of subscription services similar to D23
Pitch a
redesign
of the D23 Membership Information page based on findings
Competitive Analysis
My design partner and I did an in-depth analysis of internal Disney and indirect external subscription competitors. Please contact me at
carolyn@diloreto.com
to view this deck.
Paint Points
We identified several paint points of the D23 site that was live at the time to see what opportunities we had to improve user experience.
Process

After pitching our competitive analysis and then meeting with the marketing team to understand  the priorities for membership conversion, my design partner and I collaborated on a few versions of wireframes. Our takeaways from our analysis were to focus the audience toward new members who aren't familiar with the brand, make the CTA's clearer (through size, positioning, and white-space), prioritize content by making the video top-priority, break the D23 benefits into digestable chunks,  and use iconography and lifestyle photography to exhibit the brand and comprehensively display benefit information. After finalizing a layout, we produced a few variations of "re-skins" for specific D23 campaigns.
Result
Click here
to view live project.

Em/body explores how to motivate movement from individuals through visual and auditory reward. Using Kinect infrared sensors, an Arduino, Processing, and Max MSP, a user’s position and movement engage in versatile interactions with light and sound. These interactions progress as the user discovers and advances through stages of involvement within the sphere. Increasingly complex colors, rhythms, and sonic effects respond to various and specific body position and motion behaviors, influenced by Rudolf Laban’s movement analysis. Em/body invites the user to explore and play, ultimately developing a higher level of consciousness of the user’s own kinesthetics.
amüse

is an augmented reality app that integrates music into your everyday world. It was the Kickstarter Prize Winner for Hack Music LA 2017, a 2-day creative hackathon sponsored by LA Philharmonic. Created by Carolyn DiLoreto, Grant Singer, Emily Halaka, Molly Turner.
Hackathon Pitch
amüse

was founded on the notion that everyone can be a musician. We wanted to empower users to see the world in a different and creative way through technology. From sparking the curiosity of elementary schoolers to engaging experimental composers and audiophiles,
amüse
makes music education and creation accessible to anyone with a smartphone.
The core music theory concept that drives
amüse
is the idea of translating physical distance to a musical interval.
amüse

will find the longest parallel lines on the screen, measure the distance between them, then translate that to musical intervals. There's also opportunities for many other ways to translate visual input into sound—like colors to harmony, shape to meter, and patterns to rhythm!

In order to prove that it was technologically viable, I attempted to use code to process images in the few hours I had left at the hackathon. Using the OpenCV library in Processing, I was able to detect lines in an image. From there, I adjusted the code so that it would only display the most prominent parallel lines.

The opportunities for monetization include: in-app storage for people who want to save recordings (kids, music-makers), creating a library of sounds (think filters, sample packs), and having a premium educational mode (with theory packs).
Through amuse, we hope to expand accessibility and interest in music, enabling people to see their world in a creative way.

After the launch of the new NBC App, NBC Entertainment announced that they would make all content on this service available to NBCUniversal employees. At my time at NBC, I was tasked to create the tutorial video that walks  employees through obtaining access to the NBC app using their employee credentials. This was an interal project that I worked collaboratively with various departments to execute. The challenge to producing this video was making clear steps and visualizations that make this process seem as simple as possible. This began with conducting a competitive analysis on tutorial videos that other digital products have created, such as Spotify, Apple, Asana, and Roku. See my key takeaways below:

image.title: NBC MVP Tutorial Video - Competitive Analysis
image.description: NBC MVP Tutorial Video - Competitive Analysis

content.value: Once I gained an understanding of visual clarity and timing, I worked with the Product team to refine the necessary steps to successfully log-in with the employee credentials. I simultaneously collaborated with another designer to determine the visual aesthetic of the video, which incorporated the NBC app color schemes and simplistic text and iconography. I then storyboarded the flow of the tutorial video, outlining how I will use movement to visualize the process. Once the storyboard was approved, I gathered all necessary screen-recordings on the various platforms and built two tutorial videos with After Effects (for mobile and TV). It took multiple iterations to polish the pacing of both the copy and UI movement so that it flowed comprehensibly for the audience. These videos were then sent out to NBCUniversal employees via email. Through this process, I learned how to own a design problem that required internal collaboration along every step of the way, as well as exerise empathy in video tutorial creation.

'Shed' explores how dance can manifest the conflict of identity and sexuality in contrast to social expectations of womanhood. 2nd Place at Feminist Media Festival 2018 at USC. Official selection for San Francisco Dance Film Festival 2018, Dance Camera West 2018, Vancouver Festival of Recorded Movement 2018.

Plantum is a mobile game I created with a partner as a final project for my Mobile Games course for my Computer Programming minor at USC, using Unity and C# programming. An environmentalist from the 80’s wakes up in the future and needs to save his planet by cultivating futuristic plants, while being plummeted by space junk.
Video created for LA Contemporary Dance Company, BODYTRAFFIC to promote their company.

As a DIRECTV’s Experience Design intern, I worked alongside the Preventative Care Team to address ways to reduce customer service calls by improving aspects of the user experience. During the scope of this project, our team compiled research, ideation, and strategy for the solutions to be implemented in our next generation products.
Scope

Our project scope involved examining the current landscape of future care both competitively and within the company, establishing themes that are important in addressing CX in the future, and identifying opportunities with proposed solutions. Our end goal was to create improved experiences for DIRECTV customer service.
Research
We began this project by doing a
competitive analysis

on not only our direct competitors, but other tech companies within the entertainment industry. We identified pain-points and hero moments that we personally experienced, and eventually narrowed these heuristic categories:
Findability
Clear information architecture
Step by step process
Individual Contextualization
Preventative Support
Multiple Last Resort Options
We also researched the
future possibilities
of customer care that may be possible for DIRECTV to consider in the future, in terms of self-healing mechanisms, augmented reality, and support systems that are aware of your process.
From there, our team stepped further into the research process by
conducting interviews

on a case-by-case basis with current DIRECTV customers experiencing issues with their service. We went out into the field and joined DIRECTV technicians to customer homes and asked the following questions:
What is the problem? How did you find out about this problem, and has this problem happened before?
What was your first approach to solving this problem?
What was your troubleshooting experience like?
How did you schedule your appointment with the technician? How was this experience?
What was your experience the day of your appointment? Did you feel you were in the loop about your technician’s arrival and job ETA? Did you feel comfortable with your technician?
From these interviews, we ranked our customer’s perception on reoccurring categories:
Themes
After our research, we extracted themes that would guide ideation for future customer care experiences, specifically within the appointment process.
User Journey

We created a user journey of a typical customer who would go through a set-top-box issue, allowing us to identify opportunities of improvement of the current CX process. Opportunities are highlighted in purple.
Deliverables

Our final deliverable was a strategy pitch deck containing four ideas for solutions moving forward in improving the current CX process, identified below. Although these solutions were not immediately implemented during our internship, our research and analysis was a starting point for collaboration between the Experience Design and Preventative Care teams.
In-App Streaming

Our first idea addressed the opportunity of connecting users to entertainment even when their satellite connection is down. We realized when interviewing customers that a lot of them were not aware of DIRECTV’s app, which allows them to stream shows while they are in their own home. Knowing this could have ameliorated their issue, because they still could have watched their show even when their connection was down. We identified this as low-hanging fruit that could easily be solved if their set-top-box notified them of alternate ways of viewing their channels. Taking it even further, their app could cast their show onto their television, running off free AT&T data to appease the customer.
Themes: Transparency, Entertaining CX, Personalization
AR Troubleshooting

Looking into the future of care, we constructed an idea of how augmented reality can enhance the troubleshooting experience. In this idea, the customer can open an app on their phone that would be able to identify physical problems with the set-top-box and give the user immediate step-by-step instructions on how to fix it.
Themes: Transparency, Approachability, Entertaining CX
Auto-Scheduling

We found that many people would not fix their issue unless it surpassed a threshold of annoyance, like their favorite show suddenly going down. Currently, the only way to schedule an appointment was if they walked through the troubleshooting process with the rep on the phone, which they might have done on their own, and the rep confirms that it is a problem that needs an appointment with a technician. Ideally, the set-top-box could recognize the problem on its own and ping the customer’s phone to schedule an appointment. This solution would eliminate repetitive steps that customers inevitably take, provide a visual and more convenient way to schedule, and quicken the CX process. This solution would reduce the amount of calls to customer service, thus eliminating costs.
Themes: Transparency, Personalization, Approachability
Tech ETA

Another major pain-point that we addressed was that customers not being kept in the loop in regards to technician arrival time and job completion. In addition, some felt a bit anxious with a stranger entering their home. The customer would receive a ping on their phone with an arrival time and a photo and bio. Ideally, the customer would also be notified about the progress of the technician’s job.
Themes: Transparency, Entertaining CX, Personalization
All graphics were made by yours truly!
Problem
How can Green Dot administrative staff maintain communication with Green Dot alumni attending college in order to decrease drop-outs?
Solution

Create an application that creates a community for Green Dot alumni that the administration can push user-specific updates as well as gamify the college experience to encourage participation in alumni responses.
Team members
1 project manager
2 designers
5 developers
Process

Our process included many meetings with the client to determine what features were needed for the mobile application and the administrative website, as well as working with students to understand what their needs were. Working with an agile work flow, we went through many iterations of flows, wireframes, visual identity, and mock-ups.
Interviews

We conducted a focus group with 11 recently graduated Green Dot alumni to get an understanding of their relationship with Green Dot while a student and how Green Dot can help them succeed in their college experience. We also asked for feedback on the usefulness of features that the client and our team ideated for the app. Through these interviews we learned that gamifying the experience would lead to more interaction/communication with Green Dot.
Visual Identity
Starting with Green Dot's given branding, we replicated their fonts and chose colors that would give the app a consistent feel.
Login
Because we only want users that have pre-existing Green Dot email addresses to be able to access the features of the app, we created a multi-step process to create and verify accounts.
Notifications

Because one of the client’s biggest need was to notify alumni on various topics, notifications became a large part of our app. Organized into different categories, users would receive notifications and be able to access their history and take action on specific notifications.
Alumni Profile
In order to allow Green Dot alumni to fully benefit from the Green Dot network, we created public profiles for each user, of which they can update and edit preferences.
Alumni Search
Alumni can   search for specific users that may have been fellow classmates of there’s, or connect with alumni that go to their school. This encourages the Green Dot network to flourish.
Roadmap

To address the goal of encouraging alumni to stay in touch with the Green Dot administration, we created a gamified experience called the College Roadmap. Prompting users to fill out “checkpoints,”
alumni receive points that will accumulate to winning a prize determined by the administration at the end of each year. This allows Green Dot to be aware of what support the alumni need in order to encourage them to stay in college.
Admin Website

Using Django, we created a portal for the Green Dot administration to view and manage data for the Alumni application. Here, they can organize alumni in their system, create new notifications, and create new checkpoints for the Roadmap.
Problem
How can Chrysalis, a social enterprise that help newly homeless individuals find work, automate their process of matching their clients to job opportunities?
Solution
Create a client and employee-side web application to obtain weekly availability of clients and filter through availability and qualifications to match them with job opportunities.
Team Members
1 project manager
2 designers
5 developers
User Flow
This flow illustrates the pain points of their previous system, which involved their clients leaving weekly voicemails that arweree manually sorted through at the beginning of the week.
Client-Side Mockups
Because most of their clients had access to smartphones, we created a webform where clients could log in based on their contact information and update their availability for every week.
Employee-Side Mockups

The most important feature for the employee-side was ameliorating filtration through their clients availabilities/attributes so they could easily match their clients with employers. We created a homepage that was similar to their original organizational structure, to ease their transition. We also created a database structure to organize all their client information.
Impact

Through our work, we have helped a team of over 30 employees who support hundreds of job-seeking individuals per week. Coding experience made designing more effective, as there were many iterations I had to make that would ease the development process as we approached deadlines. Taking it further, it would be increasingly impactful to have the opportunity to integrate our service across all the three Chrysalis offices in LA.

"Huntress" is a motion capture dance piece I created using OptiTrack motion capture with virtual camera as well as keijiro's Skinner in Unity. This was my final project for Motion Capture class at USC taught by John Brennan. Performed by Celine Kiner.
